title: Your first variable
instruction:
`let` creates a variable. By default it is immutable — you cannot change it after creation.

```rust
let x = 5;
```

Run this program.
successMessage: let binds a name to a value. Rust calls this a 'binding'. The value is locked in — immutable by default.

title: Immutable means immutable
instruction:
Try to change an immutable variable and see what Rust says.

Run this program — it will fail to compile. Read the error carefully.
hint: You cannot reassign an immutable variable. Rust will refuse to compile this. Read the error message.

codeChecks.message: Notice that Rust won't let you reassign x. Add 'mut' to the let binding to fix it.

successMessage: That error message is Rust protecting you. Unintended mutation is a huge source of bugs. Rust forces you to be explicit.

title: Making a variable mutable
successMessage: mut signals intent: 'this value will change'. When you see mut, you know to pay extra attention to where and how it changes.

title: Shadowing
instruction:
Rust lets you declare a new variable with the same name — this is called *shadowing*.

```rust
let x = 5;
let x = x + 1; // new x, shadows the old one
```

Shadowing is different from mutation: you are creating a *new* variable, not changing the old one.

Run this.
successMessage: Shadowing lets you transform a value while keeping the name. Unlike mut, you can even change the type when you shadow.

title: Shadowing changes type
instruction:
One power of shadowing: the new variable can be a different type.

With `mut` you cannot change the type — only the value. With shadowing you can change both.

Run this.
successMessage: spaces started as a string slice and became a usize. With mut you'd need two different names. Shadowing lets you reuse the same name across a transformation.

title: Constants
instruction:
Constants are like variables, but:
- They are *always* immutable — no `mut` allowed
- The type *must* be annotated
- They live for the entire program duration
- Named in SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE by convention

```rust
const MAX_SCORE: u32 = 100;
```

Run this.
successMessage: Notice the underscore in 100_000 — Rust lets you use _ as a visual separator in numbers. MAX_POINTS reads the same as 100000 but is far easier to scan.

title: Declare first, use later
instruction:
You can declare a variable and assign it later — useful when the value depends on a condition.

But Rust will refuse to compile if you use a variable before assigning it.
successMessage: Rust tracks whether every variable is initialised before use. This prevents the 'use uninitialised variable' class of bugs that plague C and C++.

title: Type annotations
hint: Change 'let age =' to 'let age: i32 =' and 'let name =' to 'let name: &str ='
successMessage: Explicit types make code self-documenting. You'll add annotations when inference gets ambiguous or when you want to be crystal clear about intent.
